questions.question: Explain the working of a multi-tape Turing machine with a neat diagram.
keyPoints: Definition: Has multiple tapes each with independent head
Transition depends on k symbols (one per tape)
Can write on all tapes and move each head independently
More powerful in practice but equivalent in computability

questions.question: Define a Universal Turing Machine and explain how it simulates other Turing machines.
keyPoints: UTM can simulate any arbitrary TM
Takes two inputs: encoding of M and encoding of w
Interprets the description of M and performs same computation
Foundation of stored-program computers

questions.question: Differentiate between deterministic and non-deterministic Turing machines.
keyPoints: Deterministic: single transition per state-symbol pair
Non-deterministic: set of possible transitions
NTM accepts if SOME path accepts
Both have same computational power

questions.question: What is the Busy Beaver function? Explain Σ(2).
keyPoints: Σ(n): maximum symbols an n-state TM can write before halting
For n=2 states, Σ(2) = 4
4 states can write at most 4 symbols before halting
Grows faster than any computable function

questions.question: Explain the enumeration Turing machine with its significance.
keyPoints: Enumerates strings in a language one by one
Outputs all valid strings (may run forever)
Language is recursively enumerable iff enumerated by some TM
Used to characterize RE languages

questions.question: What is a computation history of a Turing machine? How is it used in proving undecidability?
keyPoints: Complete sequence of configurations from start to halt
Shows step-by-step execution of TM on input
Used to construct self-referential arguments
Key tool in proving halting problem undecidability

questions.question: Explain the Church-Turing thesis and its implications.
keyPoints: Any intuitively computable function is Turing-computable
TM serves as formalization of algorithm concept
All reasonable computation models are equivalent
Provides foundation for computability theory

questions.question: Design a multi-tape Turing machine to recognize the language L = {ww | w ∈ {0,1}*}. Explain the algorithm and show that it accepts palindromes in O(n) time.
keyPoints: Use 3 tapes: input, reverse, and comparison tape
Step 1: Copy input from tape 1 to tape 2 and reverse it
Step 2: Move both heads from start comparing symbols
Accept if all pairs match, reject otherwise
Time complexity: O(n) vs O(n²) for single-tape

questions.question: Explain how a Universal Turing Machine works. Show how the encoding of a TM is done and how the UTM uses this encoding to simulate the given TM.
keyPoints: UTM takes two inputs: <M> (encoding of TM) and <w> (input)
Encoding: Represent states, symbols, transitions in binary
UTM interprets <M> to understand transition rules
Applies same transitions to <w> on its tape
Reproduces exact computation of M on w

questions.question: Prove that every non-deterministic Turing machine can be simulated by a deterministic Turing machine.
keyPoints: Simulate NTM using breadth-first search on computation tree
Explore all possible transitions systematically
Maintain a queue of configurations to explore
If any branch accepts, accept; if all reject, reject
Time complexity increases but computability preserved

questions.question: Explain the differences between recursive and recursively enumerable languages. Show how Turing machines recognize each type.
keyPoints: Recursive: TM halts on ALL inputs (decider)
RE: TM halts only on accepted strings, may loop on others
For recursive languages, TM always gives yes/no answer
For RE languages, TM accepts yes but may loop on no
Every recursive language is RE but not vice versa
